en: Avetta PQF - Training and Competency

Clients want to understand if workers being asked to attend their worksites have the skills to deliver the company work scope, ensuring individual workers can deliver their work in a safe way and not hurt themselves or others present in the same location. The company should provide training and guide workers on workplace safety, identifying hazards, managing workplace risk, working with plant and equipment and hazardous materials in a structured way.\nThe HSE training can be delivered in-house or by other companies. The trainers should be competent and understand the subjects they are teaching, training should be structured,  relevant and documented (records on subjects covered, dates delivered, if training has a timeline and has to be re-visited  and who delivered the session), it is essential workers receiving any type of HSE training acknowledge and demonstrate they attended the training, this can be demonstrated with an attendance sheet which requires a signature or photo or biometric attendee format, and they should also confirm understanding of content forming part of the formal record keeping. It is a client expectation all HSE training should be provided at NO cost to the worker. If training is provided but the worker has to contribute financially, there is an option to confirm that training is provided and the worker has to contribute, use the no option.\n
Does your company provide formal training programmes  covering health, safety and environmental (HSE) practice in the workplace that is provided to workers free of charge?
Yes
No

The client is seeking to understand if the employment status of a worker will change how the company evaluates and delivers HSE training and how it is provided to the different types of workers found in a company.
The type of HSE training programme offered may vary based on the employment status of an individual, e.g.,  the administration, availability of certain training programmes, if it is provided for free or there is a charge.

Full time company employees only
Full time and part-time workers employed by the company
Subcontractor workers – all courses provided by the company are accessible / available
Subcontractors – specified courses / training subject matter only
Employment Agency workers - Limited scope of training (Covers limited training subject matter)

Labour only / Contingent labour / Day labour workers (Semi-skilled)
Other
Other

If the company does not provide any HSE training in the form of a programme, the company should confirm using the range of options to explain the reasoning behind this decision. The term formal confirms that training is delivered in a structured manner, records are kept of all training that is provided, it should not be a random activity that cannot be verified in any capacity. It is recognised that some companies are very small and as such are heavily reliant on the client or perhaps industry trade body dictating what is required. Irrelevant of this issue, it should be recognised that all companies have to ensure workers are safe in the workplace, these are responsibilities of an employer, even if the company only employs a small number of workers. If the company engages large numbers of self-employed workers, temporary day labour or employment agency there is still a responsibility to provide a safe working environment and ensure these workers have received training that will keep them safe. If the company charges workers for HSE training this should be confirmed.
Please confirm why the company does not have a formal training programme (provided free of charge), covering health, safety and environmental (HSE) practice in the workplace:
Company do not believe there is a need for a formal HSE training programme based on current scope of work
Company deliver an informal HSE training programme
Other
Company do not believe they are big enough to justify a formal HSE training programme based on current scope of work
Country we operate within does not require provision of HSE training to workers
We provide HSE training to full time workers only, they pay or contribute to the training programme
We do not provide HSE training to self-employed workers, we believe they are responsible for their own HSE training
We do not provide HSE training to temporary day workers or employment agency workers  we believe they should have the necessary training and competency before attending the workplace
Company does not have the resources to be able to provide all workers with HSE training
Current company training programmes currently under review to cover HSE content – To be implemented within 3 months from date of completing this form
Company rely on their client’s HSE training  programme
Other

This information should confirm what type of HSE training is provided to workers.
Information can be in the form of a matrix or simple spreadsheet, it can be a manual record, or list of all the topics covered,  the review process is not making any judgement, it is simply confirming HSE training is undertaken. The client would expect the most basic of issues should be covered to provide all levels of worker with guidance on what programmes are delivered for workers to stay safe,  before they start work at their sites.
It can include content such as site based training, e.g., how to wear, store and maintain personal protective equipment, using rough terrain fork truck or scissor lift. The records or information should list that provides a list of what type of training is given, date it is to be delivered and if it is an industry recognised programme.
If the approach to providing HSE training to non-employees, individual workers who have been engaged to deliver services on behalf of the company this information should also be provided, if the same format is used as full time workers this should be confirmed. If the application of HSE training is different when applied to non-employees, the information provided should confirm how this group of workers is covered, if they do attend the worksite and they already possess some form of historical training, it should be confirmed how this training is verified and confirmed as being suitable before they are allowed to work on  behalf of the company.
Upload supporting evidence to confirm what HSE training programmes are provided to the workforce and how this may  be different if the worker is not employed directly or via a subcontractor company.

Not every work site is the same, even in the same industry or when working for the same client, each site has its own risks, layouts, and site-specific demands. It is essential that all workers undergo a form of site induction / orientation so that they understand not only the company rules but should include the attitude and motivation of the senior management team to safe working practice and meeting the client demands for workplace safety and good environmental practice.\nIn the case of subcontractors who have also been engaged by the company to help deliver your work scope, it is important they also attend so that they can reflect on what is expected of them from a workplace safety and environmental point of view. In some cases, workers may have to receive an internal induction and a separate client induction. This is acceptable but it should be confirmed how the different aspects of induction are managed within the business.\n
Does the company undertake any form of HSE induction / orientation training before workers start work on site?
Yes
No

It is important that clients understand who attends HSE inductions, some companies when they engage subcontractors or self-employed workers include these workers in their induction, some companies insist these types of workers take responsibility for attending and understanding site based HSE information themselves. You should confirm what the policy is within the company and how you manage who attends HSE inductions. It may be possible the reason for this is that the time taken to attend the sessions is not included in their charges and this attendance becomes an extra charge. You can select the different options based upon the category of worker you engage to help deliver the company work services.
Confirm what groups of workers the (HSE site based) inductions / orientation training applies to:
Full time site-based workers
Temporary day labour

Volunteers attending work sites

Sub-Contractors personnel / workers
Self-employed worker engaged directly
Employment agency workers

It is essential that the company uses a range of methods to deliver the HSE induction, individuals all have different learning abilities and site conditions can vary by location, risk, and work activity. Site-based risk may require a different approach to how the message should be delivered. There is not a right or wrong response; it simply confirms that as a company there is a range of teaching methods available to ensure the most effective method can be used, which is why as many options being used can be selected.\nHaving an ability to provide a range of options means the worker has exposure to a good range of training and communication options, to ensure they understand the safety requirements of individual client sites.\n
Confirm the methods used to deliver HSE induction / orientation training:
Classroom communication and  training - no test and sign to confirm attendance
Classroom communication and training - Requires test and sign to confirm attendance
Worker completes a self-test via multimedia / eLearning - remote
Worker completes a self-test via multimedia / eLearning  at client site
Worker shown video only – worker signs to confirm attendance
Classroom guidance and Site walk around – Sign to confirm attendance
Classroom guidance and Site walk around only - no signature required
Self-assessment testing at client site - worker completes interactive assessment (Computer based)
Other
Other

It is important that clients understand why a decision has been made to not provide workers or specific categories of worker with no form of HSE induction. There may be several reasons behind the decision, and it is important this is made clear so that if required support may be needed to help address potential gaps in how the HSE message is communicated, this is irrelevant of how the worker has been engaged as pushing a consistent approach to workplace safety and good environmental practice is essential.  The approach is important especially if reliant on the client, they need to understand the message is delivered in a easy to understand format, if certain categories of worker are not included, how are they advised of safety and environmental related issues?
Confirm why the company offers no HSE induction / orientation training to workers:
Company do not believe (HSE) induction / orientation needed based on work provided
Company do not believe they are big enough to justify delivering (HSE) induction / orientation training programmes
Company does not have the resources to be able to provide a HSE induction / orientation programme
Current company HSE and training management programme currently under review with view to implement changes to current practice
Company always use client programmes
Company currently developing HSE induction / orientation training and it will be implemented within the next 3 months
Other
Other

Any form of HSE induction can be used, this is not a judgement on its suitability but a confirmation that it is in place and the range of subjects that are flagged. Actual examples can be used but it is essential that if using examples that have been used that any personal detail should be covered up or removed where possible.
Please upload an example of the HSE  induction / orientation agenda used by the company.

Not every work site is the same, even in the same industry or when working for the same client, each site has its own risks, layouts, and site-specific demands. It is essential that all workers undergo a form of site induction / orientation so that they understand not only the company rules but should include the attitude and motivation of the senior management team to safe working practice and meeting the client demands for workplace safety and good environmental practice. In some countries the briefing process may have a different title, for example it may be called a tool-box huddle, it is irrelevant what it is called, the expectation is around providing workers with relevant safety information that ensures their personal safety before work starts, this should be considered when answering the question.\nIn the case of subcontractors who have also been engaged by the company to help deliver your work scope, it is important they also attend so that they can reflect on what is expected of them from a workplace safety and environmental point of view. In some cases, workers may have to receive an internal induction and a separate client induction. This is acceptable but it should be confirmed how the different aspects of induction are managed within the business.\n
Does your company provide tool box talks / safety briefings?
Yes
No

Not all individuals who receive training and guidance have the same ability to learn and absorb information, this can be because of language skills, literacy, individual who don’t have the country language as a first language. The methods used will help to determine the effectiveness of how this message and information is received and understood. Some programmes may simply talk around the issues, some may expect workers to pass a test based on the message provided. This section allows different options to be selected based upon the methods that are used by the company.
Planning and delivery of tool box talks / safety briefings covers:
Company confirms workers understand local language to ensure understanding of content delivery
Company has a process that confirms which workers have received the briefing/guidance, e.g., sign register
Company evaluates the content and delivery process to ensure effective communication of site -safety guidance
Company uses the services of a translator / supervisor with the same language as the workers
Company uses materials written in languages spoken by the workers
Company provides  separate programmes that caters for workers who do not have local language understanding
Company uses an approach ensuring workers with literacy problems receive training messages in an understandable way
Other
Other

It is important that clients understand who attends site-based toolbox talks or briefings, some companies when they engage subcontractors or self-employed workers include these workers in their briefing exercises and can include workers from other contractors who may be working in the same area as company workers and may be impacted by the work taking place.\nSome companies insist these self-employed and subcontract workers attend site briefings and use documents to ensure they sign to show they have been briefed on site specific safety or environmental matters. Confirm what the policy is within the company and how you manage who attends toolbox talks or briefings. You can select the different options based upon the category of worker you engage to help deliver the company work services.\n

The approach is important especially if reliant on the client, they need to understand the message is delivered in a easy to understand format, if certain categories of worker are not included, how are they advised of safety and environmental related issues?

Actual examples can be used but it is essential that if using examples that have been used that any personal detail should be covered up or removed where possible.
Please upload a sample tool box talks / safety briefing that includes how your company confirms worker attendance and how the message was communicated.

When an individual comes from a different country they may have an ability to communicate but their understanding of the  main language used in the country of operation may differ by individual, also a factor not always considered is that the culture relating to workplace risk and safety can differ by country, it is essential that the message is communicated in a manner that can be understood  and they can relate  to the message being transmitted. The issue of literacy also needs to be factored into how the message is communicated , especially if slides and handouts are issued, it cannot be assume that all workers have the ability to read and write in either the main localised language or even if born in the country of operation, they may have limited  literacy skills because their current work does not need impact on their ability to read / write. The expectation is to ensure that the HSE training programme is simply not applied as a ‘one-size’ fits all, the effectiveness of how this message is delivered has to be factored in, as a company providing training to a range of worker, how is the issue managed to ensure it is delivered effectively so that all attendees leave the programmes understanding the intent and commitment of the company to a safe working environment. \n
Does the company provide health and safety training to workers that do not speak local language / and or have limited literacy?
Yes
No
N/A

To ensure the effectiveness of communicating the safety message to workers who may have limited language capabilities or may have come from countries where the issue of risk and workplace safety may be looked at and perceived very differently, the same for literacy for workers who can talk in the local language but are unable to read any form of material in the primary language of the country of operation. Literacy is not restricted to workers from other countries, it can also apply to workers from the country of operation who may have literacy issues because it does not impact upon their ability to deliver their work, it has to be innovative and effective, so they understand the commitment to providing a safe working environment and that they have an individual level of responsibility to participate in safe working practice.
Confirm methods used to communicate health and safety information to workers with  limited language skills or limited literacy:
Use translator when delivering all training courses
Use supervisory personnel to deliver training message with different language skills
Use of subtitles (alternative languages made available) on training materials
Use client resources to deliver this training
Deliver verbal only guidance / testing for workers with literacy issues
Other
Other

The retention of records for all categories of HSE training that has been provided to workers is essential for many reasons. Workers can demonstrate they have been provided with a range of training and guidance on workplace safety and the requirements needed to operate working practice safely, it can be assessed by the client or members of the senior management team within the company, it can also be a factor in the event of any form of accident or incident investigation as the type or lack of training may have been a factor. Some work roles may require a specific form of training for that worker to be classed as competent to deliver that function, the records will also show if the training has an expiry date and if the worker must receive training updates, sometimes called refresher training. Records may be in hard copy such as paper or certificates, they can be electronic, the question is not evaluating what the most suitable methodology is, it is simply seeking to understand how the company records when HSE training, irrelevant of the type of training is documented. Finally, are records maintained in a confidential manner because in some cases they may contain personal information about the individual.
Does the company manage and retain individual training records for workers?
Yes
No

To demonstrate supporting information that relates to how you have answered the question. The information is to confirm there is a process to record training provided to workers, how this is done is up to an individual company, clients expect as a minimum that certain content is retained, such as the name of the workers, the training content provided, who provided the training and the date it was delivered and if it generated any form of certification and if it was job related. Some training courses may require refresher training if this is the case how is this documented to ensure re-training is undertaken in a timely manner, especially if related to job competency.

Please upload copy of the company HSE training register (Personal details redacted) or a blank training record to confirm the format used by the company to demonstrate a record of what training has been provided.

The company competency management programme  should be different from the training programme, they are two very different things. Simply because an individual receives training, does not make them competent or increase their level of safety awareness. The competency programme is an evaluation of a person's skills and abilities. \nThe company competency process should assess the workers current  capability against the capabilities the worker needs to carry out their work  role and this includes HSE requirements as to how this relates to them performing their work. The programme should include three elements which are: core competencies, cross functional competencies and functional competencies. This follows best practice, how it is discharged is individual for each company, this can be a manual process, it may be undertaken in the work site or classroom. As a company, you may use the following processes: Worker Self-Assessment, Colleague or supervisor feedback, simulation exercises, client feedback, trainer feedback and ongoing observations. If these programmes are undertaken in addition to the delivery of the training programme, consideration should be given to how this is documented  and recorded so areas that require improvement are clear and defined.\n

Does your company competency management programme contain on-going assessments to evaluate individual workers competence in relation to the application of safe working practice and their understanding and control of risk?
Yes
No

Training needs / competencies are recorded in a matrix or similar format
There is a date based programme that confirms when refresher competency assessment is required

Clients want to understand the level of competency of workers attending their sites and working on or around external and internal infrastructure, plant and equipment.  It is essential that workers not only understand how plant and equipment works but also if their work interfaces with the infrastructure that they have been provided with relevant training so that their actions do not impact upon workplace safety and business efficiency. 
In addition to the provision of training, the company should assess the worker to verify they are competent post training.
In some cases, if specialist equipment is used by the client, items such as standard operating procedures (SOP), simulation exercises, etc, may be used. This question is seeking to establish worker training covering client based requirements when working on or around infrastructure within the client work site.

Does the company work with the client to provide on the job training to ensure workers understand how to operate/ work adjacent to  plant, equipment and process equipment infrastructure owned by the client?
Yes
No
N/A
